expectations.text: A test for the combined vip=True and code='SAVE10' case is written and confirmed to fail before any fix is applied
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 1 shows `test_vip_with_save10_code_gets_only_10_percent_discount` added and the failing output: `FAILED test_discount.py::test_vip_with_save10_code_gets_only_10_percent_discount — AssertionError: assert 80.0 == 90.0` with `1 failed, 3 passed in 0.05s` before any fix.

expectations.text: The fix is minimal — only the double-discount logic is changed
expectations.evidence: discount.py replaces the two additive `discount += 0.10` branches with a single `if vip or code == 'SAVE10': discount = 0.10 else: discount = 0.0`. The return statement and function signature are unchanged. Nothing else was touched.

expectations.text: All tests pass after the fix
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 2 shows all 4 tests passing: `test_no_discount_for_regular_customer PASSED`, `test_vip_gets_10_percent_discount PASSED`, `test_save10_code_gets_10_percent_discount PASSED`, `test_vip_with_save10_code_gets_only_10_percent_discount PASSED` — `4 passed in 0.01s`.

expectations.text: No unrelated behaviour was changed alongside the fix
expectations.evidence: The three pre-existing tests (no discount, vip-only, SAVE10-only) all continue to pass after the fix, confirming their behaviour is preserved. Only the double-discount path was altered.

expectations.text: The regression test name clearly identifies the bug scenario being reproduced
expectations.evidence: Test is named `test_vip_with_save10_code_gets_only_10_percent_discount` — this explicitly encodes the inputs (vip + SAVE10 code) and the expected outcome (only 10% discount), unambiguously identifying the bug scenario.

expectations.text: The regression test contains a single focused assertion that directly captures the double-discount defect
expectations.evidence: test_discount.py line 14: exactly one assertion — `assert calculate_discount(100, vip=True, code='SAVE10') == 90.0` — which directly tests the combined case and catches the 80.0 vs 90.0 double-discount defect.

expectations.text: The fix does not introduce new complexity or branches beyond what is needed to prevent the double application
expectations.evidence: The buggy code had two separate `if` statements (2 branches). The fix collapses them into one `if/else` using `or` (still 2 branches but simpler — no accumulation variable mutation). No new conditions, flags, or guard clauses were added.

suggestions: Consider verifying the pre-fix state with a separate snapshot of the original discount.py (buggy version) in the outputs dir, so graders can independently confirm the red-phase code without relying solely on the transcript narrative.
The comment in the regression test ('Bug: discount is applied twice') is helpful but could also include the expected vs actual values for immediate clarity when the test fails in future runs.
eval_feedback.overall: A clean, textbook TDD cycle. The red phase is clearly demonstrated with concrete failing output, the fix is minimal and correct, and all tests pass green. The regression test name and single assertion are well-targeted at the defect. No issues found.
